description: Apple's ATT framework required apps to ask users' permission before tracking them across other apps and websites. Consumers overwhelmingly supported it (~80%), while advertisers and Facebook/Meta strongly opposed. Only ~25% of users opted in to tracking when prompted.

key_events.description: Apple announces ATT at WWDC 2020. Privacy advocates celebrate. Facebook begins lobbying campaign with full-page newspaper ads claiming ATT will hurt small businesses.
Facebook launches 'Good Ideas Deserve to Be Found' campaign. Mark Zuckerberg calls Apple anti-competitive. Small business testimonials deployed.
iOS 14.5 launches April 2021. Only 4% of US users opt in to tracking in first week (Flurry Analytics). Consumer behavior overwhelmingly validates pro-privacy stance.
Facebook reports $10B revenue loss from ATT. Advertisers shift strategies. Meta shares drop. Consumer satisfaction with ATT remains high.
ATT becomes normalized. Opt-in rates stabilize at ~25%. Other platforms (Google) announce similar privacy frameworks. Industry standard shifts.

calibration_notes: Near-consensus consumer support (~80%) with strong elite opposition from ad industry. Unusual case where corporate vs. corporate battle (Apple vs. Facebook) aligned with overwhelming public preference. Consumer behavior (96% opt-out initially) even exceeded stated support. Tests: privacy as valence issue, corporate framing battles, revealed vs. stated preferences, industry disruption with public support.
